third_parties.notes: Maya says the assistant sent the final batch of photos around May 8.
Maya describes an agency invoice for a late-May campaign that was later rescheduled.

submissions.raw_text:
My name is Alex Rivera and I’m a freelance web designer. In early April I agreed to build a five-page marketing website for Maya Chen’s consulting business for $2,400. She paid a $1,200 deposit, with the balance due when the project was completed. The signed agreement is attached.

The intended launch was around May 20, although the contract also says the timeline depended on Maya supplying final copy and images by April 25. She did not send everything by then. I think the last major batch arrived around May 8 or May 9, but some small text changes kept coming after that.

The original job was a homepage, about page, services page, contact page, and mobile-responsive layout, with two revision rounds. During the project Maya also asked for a pricing comparison section, a newsletter signup, and several changes to the homepage design. I accepted most of those requests through WhatsApp because I wanted to keep the client happy. I probably should have documented more clearly which requests were outside the original scope.

I sent what I considered a complete staging version on June 3. Maya replied that it “looked really good overall” but gave me a list of changes. I made most of them during the following week. She later said the project was too late, that the mobile version had major problems, and that the delay had damaged a marketing campaign she planned to run.

There were some mobile issues, but I do not agree that the website was unusable. I fixed the issues I could reproduce. Maya also used images from the website in social media posts, and I believe at least part of the site was briefly published, although I cannot prove exactly what was live or for how long.

Maya now refuses to pay the remaining $1,200 and has also said she may ask for the deposit back. She says I disappeared for two weeks. That is exaggerated. I was slower for part of one week because of a family issue, but I still replied to messages and told her what was happening.

I feel like she is using the missed target date as an excuse not to pay for work she received. I am asking for the remaining $1,200. I will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials when the balance is paid. I want JuryAI to consider that much of the schedule delay came from late content, added requests, and repeated changes.

My name is Maya Chen and I run a small consulting business. In April I hired Alex Rivera to build a five-page website for $2,400. I paid the $1,200 deposit immediately — that part is not in dispute, and neither is the basic scope: homepage, about, services, contact, mobile.

What is in dispute is almost everything about how it went.

The May 20 launch date was not an “estimate” to me. I told Alex from the first call that I was running a paid marketing push in late May and the site had to be live before it started. On May 12 I emailed him asking point blank whether we were still on track for May 20, and he replied “yes, we’re on track.” I have that email. If the date was flexible in his mind, that was the moment to say so.

Alex says my content was late. Some of it was — I’ll be honest about that. The contract said April 25 and I sent most of the copy around April 26 or 27, I think, definitely before the end of the month. My assistant sent the last batch of photos later, around May 8, which I only realized when I went back through the messages. So I was part of the early delay. But a few days of late photos does not explain delivering the first full version on June 3, two weeks after the launch date he had confirmed on May 12.

On the “extra” requests: the pricing comparison section wasn’t an addition, it replaced content on the services page, and Alex told me on WhatsApp that the changes were included. I have a screenshot of him saying “no problem, that’s included.” I no longer have the full conversation — I cleared old chats when my phone storage filled up — but the screenshot is real.

The June 3 version looked fine on desktop, and yes, I told him it “looked really good overall.” On my phone it was a different story. The menu didn’t open on mobile and two pages overlapped so badly you couldn’t read them. I sent Alex a screen recording on June 5, and he replied — I have this message — “you’re right, mobile is a mess right now, give me a few days.” His own June 8 recording just shows it working on his phone.

Then he essentially vanished. Between roughly June 9 and June 22 I got two short messages. He mentioned a family issue and I’m sympathetic, but I had a campaign booked. I ended up pushing the launch entirely and I estimate the delay cost me at least three to four thousand dollars in lost client bookings, since May and June are my peak inquiry months.

I’ll also admit: I did use two of the design images in Instagram posts in early June, and the site was briefly reachable at my domain for a few days while we were testing. I wasn’t hiding a finished website from him. It was a partial, broken site that I couldn’t run a campaign on.

I don’t think I should pay $1,200 for a project delivered late, incomplete on mobile, and after his own written confirmation of the deadline. I’m willing to pay $600 to close this out, in exchange for the source files and admin credentials, because I did receive usable design work. If Alex won’t accept that, I want $400 of my deposit back and we both walk away.

claim_evidence_links.explanation: The described contract contains the core price, scope, deposit, and completion language.
The described chat export may establish when content arrived, but it is incomplete and uninspected.
The described June 3 email may establish staging delivery and Alex’s completion framing.
The described screenshot may establish Maya’s exact praise, with incomplete context.
Alex’s described screen recording may show mobile function on his device after fixes.
Maya’s described screen recording may show material mobile failures on her device.
The described Instagram screenshot may establish limited use of design assets.
The described full email thread may establish Alex’s May 12 confirmation.
The cropped screenshot may support inclusion but lacks referent and date.
The described recording may show mobile failures.
The described message may be an admission by Alex if attribution and context are accurate.
The full history is unavailable, so it cannot currently establish message frequency.
The campaign record may establish a booked campaign but not the amount or causation of lost bookings.
The Instagram screenshot may establish use of design images.
The described email may support the asserted deadline commitment.
The described recording may support incompleteness on mobile.

evidence_evidence_links.explanation: Both are described as portions of the parties’ WhatsApp conversation; inspection is needed to determine whether Alex’s export includes or omits the alleged June 6 message.
The selected export and cropped screenshot may overlap within the same WhatsApp conversation.
The recordings both concern mobile behavior, but they capture different site states on different devices and dates; they are not the same event or the same content.
Both parties describe payment records for the same $1,200 deposit.

Missing the late-May campaign window allegedly caused lost client bookings during peak inquiry months.
As a fallback walk-away result, Maya seeks return of part of the deposit because the project was late and incomplete.
damages_claims.calculation_basis: Requested fallback amount; no calculation supplied.

clarification_questions.question: Please supply the signed agreement and identify the exact wording for the May 20 date, content dependency, completion, acceptance, and credentials.
clarification_questions.reason: These terms control the deadline and payment-trigger interpretations.

clarification_questions.question: Please supply the most complete WhatsApp export available and explain its start/end dates and any gaps caused by the phone change.
clarification_questions.reason: The message history affects content timing, scope changes, communication frequency, and comparison with Maya’s screenshots.

clarification_questions.question: What exact work remained after June 3, and which requested changes were fixes, included revisions, or added scope?
clarification_questions.reason: The answer affects completion, scope, and whether payment was triggered.

clarification_questions.question: Between June 9 and June 22, on which dates did you communicate with Maya and what did you say?
clarification_questions.reason: This could resolve the material dispute over whether Alex essentially disappeared.

clarification_questions.question: Please supply the full May 12 email thread and the June 5 mobile screen recording.
clarification_questions.reason: These are decision-critical evidence for the deadline and mobile-defect claims.

clarification_questions.question: Please supply the June 6 and “that’s included” screenshots exactly as stored, including any visible metadata, and explain whether any backups or linked devices retain the surrounding chat.
clarification_questions.reason: Attribution and context are central, and the full chat is currently unavailable.

clarification_questions.question: What is the calculation for the claimed $3,000–$4,000 lost bookings, and what evidence connects the website delay to specific lost inquiries or revenue?
clarification_questions.reason: The campaign invoice alone does not establish amount or causation.

clarification_questions.question: On what dates was the website reachable at your domain, which pages were live, and did you or customers use any functionality?
clarification_questions.reason: The extent of use affects value received and completion assessment.

unresolved_uncertainties.issue: The signed agreement has not been inspected, so exact contract wording remains unavailable.
WhatsApp records are incomplete or unavailable and screenshots are uninspected.
The competing mobile recordings have not been inspected or compared.
The amount and causation of claimed campaign losses are not established.

ineligibility_reasons: Decision-critical evidence is described or unavailable, not inspected.
Neither party has confirmed the structured record.
